schools.summary: God remembers Noah: 'and God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged' — God remembering Noah and the creatures with him (this spoken 'after the manner of men,' an anthropopathy, not that God had forgotten, God forgetting nothing, but that God now turned in mercy to act for Noah's deliverance, God's 'remembering' his gracious turning to fulfill his covenant and to deliver the remnant); God remembering Noah and his covenant with him (6:18, through the long months of the flood, when it might have seemed Noah was forgotten, God remembering him, God's faithful care for his own, who are never forgotten by him, the turning point of the narrative, from judgment to deliverance); 'God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged (subsided)' (God sending a wind, as he later divided the Red Sea by a wind, to dry up and assuage the waters, the waters beginning to subside, God's command over the elements, the means by which the flood began to abate, the beginning of the earth's renewal) — Gill, Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: God remembered Noah, and every living thing, and all the cattle that was with him in the ark: and God made a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters asswaged
God remembering Noah and sending a wind to assuage the waters
God's gracious turning to deliver the remnant (an anthropopathy, not that God forgot)
the turn from judgment to deliverance

schools.summary: The sources of the flood stopped: 'the fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained' — the two sources of the flood (the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven, 7:11) now stopped (the subterranean waters ceasing to gush, the floodgates of heaven closed, and the rain restrained, the flood ceasing to increase, and the waters beginning to abate); the very sources that had been opened (7:11) now stopped (God, who had loosed the waters in judgment, now restraining them in mercy, the closing of the deep and the heavens marking the turning of the flood); 'the rain from heaven was restrained (held back)' (the forty days' rain, 7:12, ceasing, no more water coming down, with the sources stopped and the wind drying, v.1, the waters beginning to recede, the flood, having reached its term, beginning to abate at God's command) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: The fountains also of the deep and the windows of heaven were stopped, and the rain from heaven was restrained
the stopping of the deep and the windows of heaven
the sources of the flood closed at God's command
the waters beginning to abate, God restraining in mercy

schools.summary: The waters abate: 'and the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated' — the waters steadily returning (receding) from the earth, going and returning (ebbing), after the hundred and fifty days (7:24, the period of prevailing), the waters abated (decreased), the flood, having reached its height, now steadily diminishing; the waters 'returned... continually (going and returning)' (the waters receding gradually and steadily, not suddenly, the flood diminishing by degrees, God's ordered way of abating the waters, that the earth might dry and the ark settle); 'after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated' (at the close of the hundred and fifty days during which the waters prevailed, 7:24, they beginning to abate, the appointed period of the flood's height ended, the waters decreasing, and the ark soon to rest, v.4, the flood running its appointed course before abating) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: the waters returned from off the earth continually: and after the end of the hundred and fifty days the waters were abated
the waters abating after the hundred and fifty days
the flood steadily receding at God's command
the appointed period of the flood's height ended

schools.summary: The ark rests on Ararat: 'and the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat' — as the waters abated, the ark settling and resting (the date given precisely, the ark, which had floated on the prevailing waters, now coming to rest on the mountains as the waters receded); the ark 'rested' (after the long tossing on the flood, the ark coming to rest, God bringing the ark and its passengers to a settled place, the rest after the judgment, the figure of the believer's rest after the storm, brought safe by God); 'upon the mountains of Ararat' (the ark resting on the mountains of Ararat, a region in Armenia, the mountainous country to the north, the highest peak now called Mount Ararat, the 'mountains,' plural, suggesting the mountainous region rather than a single peak, the ark coming to rest on the high ground first uncovered as the waters fell, the historical location of the ark's resting) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Clarke, JFB.

themes: the ark rested in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat
the ark resting on the mountains of Ararat
the ark settled on the high ground as the waters fell
the rest after the long tossing on the flood

schools.summary: The waters decrease, the mountaintops appear: 'and the waters decreased continually until the tenth month: in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, were the tops of the mountains seen' — the waters steadily decreasing, by the first day of the tenth month the tops of the mountains becoming visible (the gradual uncovering of the earth as the flood receded, the dated progress of the drying); the waters 'decreased continually (going and decreasing)' (the steady, ordered diminishing of the flood, the waters falling by degrees, day by day, God's gradual way of drying the earth); 'were the tops of the mountains seen' (the highest points of the mountains becoming visible as the waters fell, the first sight of the emerging earth, a sign of hope to Noah that the flood was abating and the earth would be dry, the gradual reappearance of the land) — Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: the waters decreased continually until the tenth month... were the tops of the mountains seen
the waters decreasing until the mountaintops appeared
the gradual uncovering of the earth as the flood receded
the first sight of the emerging land, a sign of hope

schools.summary: Noah opens the window: 'and it came to pass at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made' — forty days after the mountaintops appeared (v.5), Noah opening the window (the opening he had made, 6:16) to test the state of the waters (he seeking to learn whether the earth was drying, Noah's careful, patient waiting and observing); Noah, having waited patiently, now opening the window to investigate (his patience through the long months, waiting on God's time, he not rushing out but waiting and testing, the patient faith of Noah, who waited for God's signal to leave the ark); 'the window... which he had made' (the window, 6:16, made for light and observation, opened, through it Noah sending out the raven and the dove, vv.7-12, to learn the state of the earth, the means by which Noah, shut in by God, 7:16, observed the abating of the waters) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: at the end of forty days, that Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made
Noah opening the window of the ark
Noah's patient testing of the state of the waters
the patient faith waiting on God's time

schools.summary: Noah sends the raven: 'and he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth' — Noah first sending out a raven to test the state of the earth (the raven 'went forth to and fro,' going out and returning, or flying back and forth, not returning to the ark to stay, the raven, a carrion-bird, finding enough — floating carcasses, or the mountaintops — to subsist without returning, it flying about until the waters dried); the raven, an unclean bird that feeds on carrion, 'went forth to and fro' (it not returning into the ark, or returning only to rest on it, finding food and footing outside, the raven's nature, content with the carcasses and the bare ground, contrasted with the dove, v.8, which sought clean rest and returned); the raven flying about 'until the waters were dried up from off the earth' (its not returning giving Noah some indication, but the raven, content with carrion and bare ground, a less sure sign than the dove, Noah sending the dove, v.8, for a surer test of the earth's dryness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: he sent forth a raven, which went forth to and fro, until the waters were dried up from off the earth
Noah sending forth the raven
the carrion-bird finding food and footing outside, not returning
the raven a less sure sign, prompting the sending of the dove

schools.summary: Noah sends the dove: 'also he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground' — after the raven, Noah sending out a dove, to test more surely whether the waters had abated from the ground (the dove, a clean bird that seeks clean rest, would return if it found no dry place, Noah using the dove as a surer means of learning the state of the earth); the dove, unlike the raven, a clean bird that loves clean, dry resting-places (the dove not lighting on carrion or bare mud, but seeking a clean perch, her behavior, returning, v.9, giving Noah a surer sign of whether the earth was dry); 'to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground' (Noah sending the dove to learn whether the ground was dry enough, the dove's return or staying indicating the state of the earth, the careful, patient testing of the waters, Noah's wisdom in using the birds to gather knowledge) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: he sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters were abated from off the face of the ground
Noah sending forth the dove to test the waters
the clean bird seeking clean rest, a surer sign than the raven
Noah's careful, patient testing of the earth

schools.summary: The dove returns for want of rest: 'but the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark, for the waters were on the face of the whole earth: then he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark' — the dove, finding no dry place to rest (the waters still covering the earth), returning to the ark (the clean bird, unable to find clean rest, coming back to Noah, the dove's return signaling that the earth was not yet dry); 'the waters were on the face of the whole earth' (the waters still covering the ground, the low places, the valleys, though the mountaintops had appeared, v.5, there as yet no dry resting-place for the dove, the earth not yet ready); 'he put forth his hand, and took her, and pulled her in unto him into the ark' (Noah gently receiving the returning dove, taking her back into the ark, his tender care for the bird, the dove finding refuge again in the ark, the dove, returning to the ark for rest, seen as a figure of the soul finding no rest but in Christ/the ark of safety) — Gill.

themes: the dove found no rest for the sole of her foot, and she returned unto him into the ark
the dove returning to the ark for want of rest
the ground still covered, no resting-place, the earth not yet dry
Noah's tender care receiving the dove into the refuge

schools.summary: Noah waits and sends the dove again: 'and he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark' — Noah waiting another seven days before sending the dove out again (the patient interval of waiting, the use of the seven-day period, perhaps marking the week, or the Sabbath rest, in Noah's testing, his patient, ordered waiting on God's time); Noah 'stayed yet other seven days' (he waiting patiently a full week before the next test, his patience and self-restraint, not rushing the matter but waiting in faith, the seven-day intervals marking his measured, patient observation of the abating flood); 'again he sent forth the dove out of the ark' (Noah sending the dove a second time, to test again whether the waters had abated, the dove now bringing back a sign, the olive leaf, v.11, the patient, repeated testing by which Noah learned the state of the earth, the renewed sending of the surer bird) — Gill, JFB.

themes: he stayed yet other seven days; and again he sent forth the dove out of the ark
Noah waiting seven days and sending the dove again
the patient, measured waiting on God's time
the renewed test of the abating waters

schools.summary: The dove returns with the olive leaf: 'and the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth' — the dove returning at evening, bearing in her mouth a freshly plucked olive leaf (the olive leaf, plucked from a tree, showing that the waters had abated enough for the trees to appear, the sign that the earth was emerging); 'an olive leaf pluckt off' freshly plucked, green (showing that the olive trees, which grow on lower ground, were now uncovered and putting forth leaves, the olive, an evergreen, recovering quickly, the olive leaf becoming the emblem of peace and of the abating of God's wrath, the dove and olive branch as symbols of peace and reconciliation); 'so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth' (by the olive leaf, Noah knowing the waters had abated and the earth was emerging, the sign giving him assurance and hope, the dove and the olive leaf bringing the glad tidings that the flood was passing and the earth would soon be dry) — Gill.

themes: the dove came in to him in the evening; and, lo, in her mouth was an olive leaf pluckt off: so Noah knew that the waters were abated from off the earth
the dove returning with the olive leaf, the sign of abated waters
the olive leaf, emblem of peace and of the emerging earth
the glad tidings that the flood was passing

schools.summary: The dove does not return: 'and he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more' — Noah waiting another seven days and sending the dove a third time (this time the dove not returning, having found dry ground and rest, she staying out, the dove's not returning the final sign that the earth was dry and habitable); Noah again 'stayed yet other seven days' (his continued patient waiting, the third seven-day interval, his measured, faithful patience in testing the earth before leaving the ark); 'which returned not again unto him any more' (the dove, finding the earth now dry and able to sustain her, not returning, her staying out the sign that the flood had abated and the earth was habitable, the dove's behavior, through the three sendings, tracing the drying of the earth — returning, no rest, v.9, returning with the leaf, waters abating, v.11, and not returning, the earth dry — the final sign of the renewed earth) — Gill.

themes: he stayed yet other seven days; and sent forth the dove; which returned not again unto him any more
the dove not returning, the sign of the dry earth
the dove finding dry ground and staying out
the final sign that the earth was habitable

schools.summary: The waters dried, Noah looks: 'and it came to pass in the six hundredth and first year, in the first month, the first day of the month, the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dry' — on the first day of the new year (Noah's six hundred and first year), the waters dried up from the surface (the precise dating marking the progress of the drying, the earth's surface now dry, though not yet fully ready, v.14); 'Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked' (Noah taking off the covering, the roof or a part of it, to look out upon the earth, beholding with his own eyes the state of the ground, his careful, patient observation before leaving, he looking and seeing); 'behold, the face of the ground was dry' (the surface of the earth now dry, the waters drained from the face of the ground, the welcome sight of the dry earth, the renewed world emerging, yet Noah still waiting, v.14, for God's command before going forth) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: the waters were dried up from off the earth: and Noah removed the covering of the ark, and looked, and, behold, the face of the ground was dry
the drying of the earth and Noah looking upon it
the surface of the earth dry, the renewed world emerging
Noah's careful looking, yet still waiting for God's command

schools.summary: The earth fully dried: 'and in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried' — by the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth fully dried (not only the surface, v.13, but the ground throughout, the earth now firm and dry, fit for habitation, the complete drying of the earth, a little over a year after the flood began, 7:11); 'the earth dried (was thoroughly dry)' (the ground now solid and dry, no longer mud or marsh, the earth fully ready to be inhabited, the distinction from v.13, the surface dry, marking the full drying of the ground); from the seventeenth day of the second month (7:11, the flood beginning) to the twenty-seventh day of the second month (the earth dried) a full year and ten days (the flood and its abating spanning about a year, God's appointed time for the judgment and the renewal, the earth, fully dried, awaiting Noah's going forth at God's command, vv.15-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: in the second month, on the seven and twentieth day of the month, was the earth dried
the full drying of the earth
the ground firm and fit for habitation, the renewed world
the flood and its abating spanning about a year

schools.summary: God speaks to Noah: 'and God spake unto Noah, saying' — God now speaking to Noah, to command him to go forth from the ark (v.16, Noah, who had waited patiently, not leaving the ark until God bade him, now receiving the word of God, the introduction to God's command for Noah to leave the ark and the renewal of the earth); Noah waiting for God to speak (though the earth was dry, v.14, Noah not going forth until God commanded, he entering the ark at God's word, 7:1, and leaving it at God's word, the patient obedience of faith, waiting on God's direction in all things); 'saying' (God's word following, v.16, bidding Noah go forth, God, who had shut Noah in, 7:16, now opening the way for him to go out, the divine command governing Noah's entering and leaving, the believer acting on God's word, Noah's patient waiting for God's signal the pattern of obedience) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And God spake unto Noah, saying
God speaking to Noah
God's word governing Noah's going forth, as it had his entering
the patient obedience of faith waiting on God's direction

schools.summary: God bids Noah go forth: 'go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives with thee' — God commanding Noah and his whole household to go forth from the ark (the eight souls who entered, 7:13, now bidden to leave, God, who had brought them in and shut them in, 7:16, now bidding them go out into the renewed earth); Noah commanded to 'go forth' (to leave the ark and enter the renewed world, he waiting for this command, not going out of his own accord, the believer leaving the place of refuge at God's word, the orderly, obedient going forth at God's command); 'thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives' (the whole family, the eight souls, to go forth together, the household preserved through the flood now going out to repeople the earth, the family of the righteous, saved together, together entering the new world, through them the race to be renewed) — Gill.

themes: Go forth of the ark, thou, and thy wife, and thy sons, and thy sons' wives with thee
God's command to Noah and his household to go forth
the household preserved through the flood bidden to leave and repeople the earth
the orderly, obedient going forth at God's command

schools.summary: Bring forth the creatures: 'bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth; that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth' — God commanding Noah to bring out all the creatures (fowls, cattle, creeping things) preserved in the ark (the animals, like the family, to go forth into the renewed earth, the preserved kinds released to repopulate the world); all the kinds of creatures preserved in the ark to be brought forth (the animal creation, saved through the flood, now going out to refill the earth, the preserved pairs released to renew their kinds); 'that they may breed abundantly... and be fruitful, and multiply' (God renewing the blessing of fruitfulness, 1:22, upon the creatures, they to breed, be fruitful, and multiply, refilling the earth, the renewal of the creation's fruitfulness after the flood, God's purpose to restock and replenish the world through the preserved kinds) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: Bring forth with thee every living thing that is with thee... that they may breed abundantly in the earth, and be fruitful, and multiply upon the earth
the command to bring forth the creatures to repeople the earth
the preserved kinds released to refill the renewed world
the blessing of fruitfulness renewed after the flood

schools.summary: Noah goes forth: 'and Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him' — Noah and his whole household going forth from the ark, as God had commanded (v.16, the eight souls, preserved through the flood, now leaving the ark and entering the renewed earth, Noah's obedience to God's command, going forth at the divine word); Noah going forth in obedience to God's command (v.16, not before, but when God bade him, the patient obedience of faith, waiting for God's word and then acting on it, Noah entering the ark and leaving it at God's direction); 'and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives' (the whole family going forth together, the eight souls who entered, 7:13, all coming out, the household of the righteous, preserved together, together entering the new world, through them the race to be renewed, the family that rode out the flood now setting foot on the cleansed earth) — Gill.

themes: And Noah went forth, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him
Noah and his household going forth from the ark
the eight souls leaving the ark at God's command
the obedient entrance into the renewed world

schools.summary: The creatures go forth: 'every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark' — all the creatures preserved in the ark (beasts, creeping things, fowls), each after its kind, going forth into the renewed earth (the animal kinds, saved through the flood, now released to repopulate the world); the classes of creatures going forth — beasts, creeping things, fowls (the comprehensive release of all the preserved kinds, the animal creation, saved in the ark, now set free upon the earth); 'after their kinds, went forth out of the ark' (the creatures going forth by their kinds, in order, the distinct kinds, the species of ch. 1, preserved through the flood and now released, the renewal of the animal creation, the kinds going out to breed and multiply, v.17, the orderly repopulation of the earth by the preserved kinds) — Gill.

themes: Every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds, went forth out of the ark
the creatures going forth from the ark after their kinds
the preserved kinds released to repopulate and renew the earth
the orderly repopulation of the earth by the kinds

schools.summary: Noah's altar and burnt offering: 'and Noah builded an altar unto the LORD; and took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl, and offered burnt offerings on the altar' — Noah's first act on leaving the ark being to build an altar to the LORD (the first altar named in Scripture, Noah, delivered from the flood, devoting his first care to the worship of God, the altar for God, an act of thanksgiving and devotion); Noah taking 'of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl' (offering the clean animals, the distinction of clean and unclean, 7:2, here serving its purpose: the extra clean beasts preserved for sacrifice, the clean creatures, fit for offering to God, used, Noah's sacrifice according to God's appointment); 'offered burnt offerings on the altar' (whole offerings, wholly consumed, signifying entire devotion and atonement, the burnt offerings expressing his thankfulness for deliverance and his devotion to God, the sacrifice, typical of the sacrifice of Christ, offered in faith, Noah's worship of the delivering God, the first recorded sacrifice after the flood) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke, Henry.

themes: Noah builded an altar unto the LORD; and took of every clean beast, and of every clean fowl, and offered burnt offerings on the altar
Noah building an altar and offering burnt offerings
the first altar, Noah's worship of the delivering God in thanksgiving
the sacrifice typical of Christ, offered in faith

schools.summary: God accepts the offering and resolves forbearance: 'and the LORD smelled a sweet savour; and the LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake; for the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth; neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done' — 'the LORD smelled a sweet savour (a savour of rest)' the accepted offering (the anthropomorphism expressing God's gracious acceptance of the sacrifice offered in faith, the offering pleasing to God, Ephesians 5:2, the figure of the sweet-smelling sacrifice of Christ, God propitiated and well-pleased); 'I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake' (God resolving not to add a further curse upon the ground, nor to repeat such a judgment, the flood, not that the curse of 3:17 is removed, but that no new curse or universal flood-judgment would come, God's gracious forbearance); 'neither will I again smite any more every thing living, as I have done' (God not again smiting all living things with such a flood, the universal judgment by water not to be repeated, the promise of forbearance, sealed by the covenant of the rainbow, 9:11-15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

Calvin and Ellicott on 'the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth': the same fact stated in 6:5 (man's depravity), which there was the ground of the judgment, here given (paradoxically) as a reason for mercy and forbearance; the phrase teaching the innate, original corruption of man (evil 'from his youth,' from his very beginning, vitiated by original sin, the doctrine of original/innate iniquity — Calvin); and God, considering man's incurable weakness and depravity, in pity resolving not to repeat the curse — the very corruption that justified the flood becoming, in God's mercy, an occasion of his forbearance, God's mercy regarding man's frailty; the same reason that grounded the flood now grounding God's pity on incurably weak man.

debates.question: How does 'the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth' function as the ground of God's resolve?

sides.position: A reason for mercy/forbearance — God, pitying man's innate, incurable depravity (original sin), resolves not to repeat the curse; the same fact that grounded the flood (6:5) now grounds his forbearance
A restatement of man's guilt/depravity (as in 6:5) — the innate corruption of man, original sin, the ground on which judgment was just
holders: the reading that stresses the statement of depravity

themes: the LORD smelled a sweet savour; and the LORD said in his heart, I will not again curse the ground any more for man's sake; for the imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth
God's acceptance of the offering and resolve never again to curse the ground
the imagination of man's heart evil from his youth (original sin) - the crux of its function
the same fact that grounded the flood now grounding God's pitying forbearance

schools.summary: The promise of the seasons: 'while the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease' — 'while the earth remaineth (all the days of the earth)' the duration (for as long as the earth endures, until the end of the world, the promised order of nature continuing, the promise covering the whole duration of the present earth, the stability of the natural order until the consummation); 'seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night' the ordered seasons (the paired opposites, the regular succession of the seasons and the day and night, the ordered round of nature — sowing and reaping, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night — continuing without interruption, the faithful regularity of the natural order, by which human life is sustained); 'shall not cease' (these never ceasing, while the earth remains, God promising the unbroken continuance of the seasons and the day and night, no more a flood interrupting the order of nature, the stability of the world the gift of God's faithfulness, the regular order of nature, on which agriculture and life depend, secured by God's promise, the covenant faithfulness of God in upholding the creation) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease
God's promise of the unbroken order of the seasons
the ordered round of nature secured by God's promise
the stability of the world the gift of God's covenant faithfulness
